CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE EDUCATION
Engage in daily physical activities, such as walking, and maintain control over blood pressure, blood
glucose, cholesterol levels, and weight.
Avoid alcohol, cigarette smoking, and NSAIDs like Advil and Aleve.
Limit dietary intake of high protein foods, saturated fats, phosphorus, potassium, and restrict sodium to
no more than 2 grams per day.
Opt for kidney-friendly foods including berries, grapes, cherries, apples, plums, cauliflower, onions,
eggplant, and turnips.
Choose lean proteins like poultry, fish, eggs, and unsalted seafood; drink water and unsweetened tea;
consume fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids.
(E11.51) Type 2 diabetes mellitus with peripheral vascular disease (CMS/HCC)
Managing your diabetes is crucial to prevent complications like nerve damage and poor blood flow.
Keep your blood sugar levels within the target range to reduce the risk of diabetic polyneuropathy.
Regular exercise and a healthy diet can help improve blood flow and manage peripheral vascular
disease.
Check your feet daily for any sores or changes, and report them to your doctor immediately.
Take your medications as prescribed and attend regular follow-up appointments to monitor your
condition.

Counseling for obesity :
Obesity is a complex disease involving an excessive amount of body fat. Obesity isn't just a cosmetic
concern.
It's a medical problem that increases the risk of other diseases and health problems, such as heart
disease, diabetes, high blood pressure and certain cancers.
The good news is that even modest weight loss can improve or prevent the health problems
associated with obesity.
A healthier diet, increased physical activity and behavior changes can help you lose weight.

Eating a healthy diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins helps maintain overall

Moderate exercise is known to reduce stress and anxiety. Aim for at least 30 minutes of moderate
exercise most days of the week.
Ensure 7-9 hours of quality sleep per night to help manage stress and reduce anxiety symptoms.
Reduce caffeine and sugar intake, as these can exacerbate anxiety symptom
Engaging in enjoyable activities and hobbies can provide a healthy distraction from stress and anxiety.

EXAM: CT SOFT TISSUE NECK WITH CONTRAST
HISTORY: Nodule of the pharynx.
TECHNIQUE: Contiguous axial images were obtained utilizing a multislice, multidetector CT scanner. Post-
processed reformations were also submitted for review. The images were obtained following the administration
of IV contrast.
Contrast: 100 cc Optiray 350 IV contrast.
The total DLP was 671.31 mGy-cm and the CTDI was 20.48 mGy.
One or more of the following dose reduction techniques were used: automated exposure control, adjustment of
the mA and/or kV according to patient size, use of iterative reconstruction technique. A total of 1 CT
(Computed Tomography) examinations and 0 myocardial perfusion studies have been performed on this patient
over the past 12 months. Counts as indicated include examinations performed within our network.
COMPARISON: CT chest from 2/19/2026.
FINDINGS: Superficial right parotid calcification. Parotid and submandibular glands are otherwise normal.
Unremarkable appearance of the thyroid gland. 36 X 30 X 48 mm enhancing mass in the left oropharynx at the
base of the tongue with extension across the midline to the right. Mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid.
Centrally hypodense 27 X 35 X 38 mm right level 2A mass. Normal opacification of the neck vessels. Cervical
spondylosis with spinal canal and neural foraminal stenosis at C5-C6 and C6-C7. No aggressive osseous
lesions. No intracranial abnormality. Imaged paranasal sinuses, mastoid air cells, and orbits are unremarkable.
IMPRESSION:
Large left tongue base mass measuring 27 X 35 X 38 mm with extension across the midline to the right,
squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise.
Presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node.
Cervical spondylosis.
Confidential
Patient:
Page 1 of 2

claude_vision.referral_reason: Urgent ENT consultation for a large left tongue base mass (36 x 30 x 48 mm) identified on CT neck with contrast, with extension across the midline to the right, abutting the epiglottis and left hyoid, and a presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node. The patient also presented with hemoptysis (coughing up blood). Diagnosis coded as D37.02 — Neoplasm of uncertain behavior of base of tongue.
relevant_clinical_findings: Chief complaint: Follow-up and coughing up blood (hemoptysis)

CT neck with contrast revealed a large left tongue base mass measuring 27 x 35 x 38 mm (also described as 36 x 30 x 48 mm in the radiology report) with extension across the midline to the right — squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise
Mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid — raising concern for airway proximity
Presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node identified on CT
Cervical spondylosis with spinal canal and neural foraminal stenosis at C5-C6 and C6-C7 (incidental finding, not directly ENT-related but noted)
Patient reports anxiousness and depressed mood — likely reactive to new serious diagnosis
No reported dysphagia, shortness of breath, chest pain, or other airway compromise symptoms documented at time of visit
Referral classified as URGENT by referring provider
Referral also placed to Oncology (external, pending review)
Patient allergy: Epinephrine — clinically relevant for any procedural planning
claude_vision.imaging_summary: CT Soft Tissue Neck with Contrast performed at DRI Elk Grove (RadNet Imaging Center), ordered by Dr. Paramjit Takhar. FINDINGS: 36 x 30 x 48 mm enhancing mass in the left oropharynx at the base of the tongue with extension across the midline to the right; mass abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid. Centrally hypodense 27 x 35 x 38 mm right level 2A mass consistent with presumed metastatic lymph node. Superficial right parotid calcification. Cervical spondylosis with spinal canal and neural foraminal stenosis at C5-C6 and C6-C7. No aggressive osseous lesions. No intracranial abnormality. IMPRESSION: Large left tongue base mass — squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise; presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node; cervical spondylosis. CT report IS included in the fax packet (pages 13 of 13 of the faxed referral).
missing_information: Biopsy or pathology results — no tissue diagnosis has been established; mass is 'squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise'

provider_urgency_label.label: urgent
provider_urgency_label.source: Multiple locations: (1) Referral Information table on page 04 of 13 lists 'Priority: Emergency'; (2) Referral letter on page 02 of 13 states '**URGENT**' in bold above the diagnosis; (3) Referral scheduling note on page 1 of document set references 'URG' attachment label; (4) Internal message visible in scheduling comments references 'urg ref expo' and 'triage from MD' handwritten annotation; (5) Referral was entered with Start Date 03/07/2026 and classified as 'Care Ctr/Clinic Action Needed'

matched_criteria: Rapidly growing neck or oral lesions
evidence: CT Impression: 'Large left tongue base mass measuring 27 x 35 x 38 mm with extension across the midline to the right, squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise. Presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node.'
Referral diagnosis: 'D37.02 (ICD-10-CM) - Neoplasm of uncertain behavior of base of tongue' — marked **URGENT** in referral letter

Mass measures up to 48 mm, abuts the epiglottis and left hyoid, and has crossed the midline — indicating a large, locally advanced lesion consistent with a rapidly progressing oral/oropharyngeal neoplasm

Visit note: 'Large left tongue base mass measuring 27 x 35 x 38 mm with extension across the midline to the right, squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise. Urgent referral to ENT and oncology submitted today.'
criteria_check.provider_label: Urgent / Emergency — marked in multiple locations including referral priority field ('Emergency'), referral letter ('**URGENT**'), and scheduling annotations ('URG', 'triage from MD')
criteria_check.reasoning: The clinical content directly matches two urgent ENT criteria — confirmed or suspected malignancy (tongue base mass, SCC until proven otherwise, with presumed metastatic lymph node) and a large rapidly growing oral lesion abutting the epiglottis — and the referring provider independently flagged this as urgent/emergency, placing both criteria and provider label in full agreement; Tier 1 is assigned.
criteria_check.recommended_window: 3-4 weeks (suspected malignancy criterion); however, given mass size, midline extension, epiglottis abutment, and presumed nodal metastasis, expedited scheduling within 1-2 weeks should be strongly considered

claude_vision.next_steps: Schedule within 3-4 weeks per suspected malignancy (tongue base SCC) criteria — however, given the large mass size (up to 48 mm), midline extension, epiglottis abutment, and presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node, the triage coordinator should consider escalating to a 1-2 week appointment; confirm that the concurrent Oncology referral has also been activated, and flag the missing medication list and tissue/biopsy status prior to the ENT visit.
claude_vision.summary: This is an urgent ENT referral for an adult male with a large left oropharyngeal tongue base mass (up to 48 mm) identified on CT neck, described as squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise, with extension across the midline, abutment of the epiglottis and left hyoid, and a presumed metastatic right level 2A lymph node. The patient presented with hemoptysis and has no tissue diagnosis yet; both ENT and Oncology referrals were submitted simultaneously on 3/7/2026. The referring provider flagged this as Emergency/Urgent priority, and the clinical findings independently meet Tier 1 urgent ENT criteria for suspected malignancy and rapidly growing oral lesion.
